title: 01 Matrix
description:
Given an `m x n` binary matrix `mat`, return the distance of the nearest `0` for each cell.

The distance between two adjacent cells is `1`.

examples.explanation: The center cell has one adjacent 0, so distance is 1.
The bottom-center cell is 2 steps away from the nearest 0.

constraints: There is at least one 0 in mat
intuition:
**The naive approach** — for each `1`, run a BFS to find its nearest `0` — is O((m·n)²), which is too slow.

**The key insight**: flip the question. Instead of asking *

*

Start a **multi-source BFS simultaneously from every `0`**. Each 0 begins at distance 0 and expands outward wave by wave. The first time a `1`-cell is reached by the BFS, that distance is guaranteed to be the minimum — because BFS explores in order of increasing distance.

This is the same trick used in **rotting oranges** and **walls and gates**: when you have multiple sources and want minimum distance to any source, seed all sources at once instead of running separate BFS per cell.

approach.name: Multi-source BFS
steps: Scan the entire matrix. For each 0-cell, set dist[i][j] = 0 and push (i, j) into the BFS queue.
For every 1-cell, set dist[i][j] = infinity initially.
Run BFS. For each cell dequeued at distance d, check its 4 neighbors.
If a neighbor's current distance > d + 1, update it to d + 1 and enqueue it.
Because BFS visits by level, the first update to any cell is always the shortest path.

mcqs.question: Why do we initialize the BFS queue with ALL 0-cells at the start, rather than running a separate BFS from each 0?
options: To reduce space complexity from O(m·n) to O(1)
Multi-source BFS gives every cell the minimum distance to ANY 0 in a single O(m·n) pass
Single-source BFS doesn't work on binary matrices
It avoids needing a visited array
mcqs.explanation: Running BFS from each 0 separately would be O((m·n)²) in the worst case. By seeding all 0s simultaneously, the BFS frontier expands outward from all sources at once — the first time any cell is reached is by definition the shortest path to any 0.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the multi-source BFS solution?
options: O(m·n·log(m·n))
O((m·n)²)
O(m·n)
O(m + n)
mcqs.explanation: Each cell is added to the BFS queue at most once (only when a shorter distance is found, and since BFS processes in order, each cell is settled exactly once). With m·n cells each doing O(1) work, total is O(m·n).

mcqs.question: What happens if you initialize dist[i][j] = 0 for all cells (not just 0s) before running BFS?
options: The algorithm still works because BFS corrects the values
All distances remain 0 — the condition dist[nr][nc] > dist[r][c] + 1 is never true for 1-cells
It causes an infinite loop
Only the 0-cells are affected
mcqs.explanation: If every cell starts at 0, then for any neighbor: 0 > 0 + 1 is false, so no cell ever gets updated. You'd return a matrix of all zeros. The infinity initialization for 1-cells is what allows BFS to propagate real distances.

mcqs.question: Which other LeetCode problem uses the exact same multi-source BFS pattern?
options: Two Sum (LC 1)
Rotting Oranges (LC 994)
Binary Search (LC 704)
Longest Common Subsequence (LC 1143)
mcqs.explanation: Rotting Oranges seeds BFS from all rotten oranges simultaneously and spreads outward — identical pattern. 'Walls and Gates' (LC 286) is another classic example of the same technique.

mcqs.question: Can this problem be solved with dynamic programming instead of BFS?
options: No — DP cannot solve shortest-path problems on grids
Yes — two passes (top-left to bottom-right, then bottom-right to top-left) correctly propagate minimum distances
Yes — but only for square matrices
No — DP requires the graph to be a DAG
mcqs.explanation: A DP solution works with two passes: first sweep top→bottom, left→right updating dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i-1][j]+1, dist[i][j-1]+1). Then sweep bottom→top, right→left for the remaining directions. Both BFS and DP give O(m·n) time.
